sections.title: What this section covers
sections.content: This topic covers **punctuation** (especially commas) and **capitalisation** in English, focusing on common mistakes.

sections.title: Commas: when to use them
sections.content: Where English wants a comma: lists, introductory phrases, before linking words joining two full sentences, and around extra information.

diagrams.title: Comma uses
rows: apples**,** oranges**,** and bananas (last comma optional in British English)
**However,** we agreed. / **After the meeting,** he left.
Before *but, and, or, so, yet* joining two full sentences
I called**,** but she was busy.
My brother**, who lives in London,** is a doctor.

items.good: The man who called yesterday left a message.
items.why: do NOT use a comma when the information is essential (defining)

sections.title: Commas: common mistakes
sections.content: Two traps: the comma before *that*, and the comma before *and/but*.

items.good: I think that he is right.
items.bad: I think, that he is right.

items.why: no comma — not two full sentences

sections.title: Capitalisation: the rules
sections.content: What English capitalises — and what it doesn't.

items.good: *university* (general noun) vs **Oxford University** (part of a name)
items.why: do **not** capitalise general nouns unless part of a name

sections.title: Capitalisation: common mistakes
sections.content: Three things that are **always** capitalised in English.

sections.title: Colons
sections.content: **Colon (:)** — used to introduce a list, explanation, or example. Also used for time.

items.label: list
items.formula: We need three things**:** time, money, and help.

items.label: explanation
items.formula: There was one problem**:** the door was locked.

sections.title: Semicolons
sections.content: **Semicolon (;)** — two jobs: joining two closely related full sentences, and separating heavy list items.

items.label: two related full sentences (instead of comma + *and*)
items.formula: It was late**;** we went home.

items.label: list items that already contain commas
items.formula: We visited Paris, France**;** Rome, Italy**;** and Berlin, Germany.

diagrams.text: Do **not** use a semicolon before a dependent clause (before *because* or *although*) — use a comma instead.

diagrams.title: Key punctuation and capitalisation rules
rows: I think that he is right.
I called, but she was busy.
We need two things: time and money.
Between two related sentences; or in lists with commas

sections.title: Reference
sections.content: For more on comma use with relative clauses, see the Relative Pronouns topic under Choose Grammar Topic.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: I think that he is right.
options.b: I think, that he is right.
options.c: I think that, he is right.
options.d: I think, that, he is right.

questions.explanation: In English we usually do not put a comma before 'that' in this type of sentence.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Days of the week are capitalised in English.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: The pronoun I is always capitalised in English. First word of sentence is capitalised.

questions.question: Which is correct? (You have more than one brother; the London one is the doctor.)

options.a: My brother, who lives in London, is a doctor.
options.b: My brother who lives in London is a doctor.
options.c: My brother, who lives in London is a doctor.
options.d: My brother who lives in London, is a doctor.

questions.explanation: When the clause is extra (non-defining) information, we put commas around it: My brother, who lives in London, is a doctor.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Languages and nationalities are capitalised in English. Sentence starts with a capital.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: We need three things time, money, and help.
options.b: We need three things: time, money, and help.
options.c: We need three things; time, money, and help.
options.d: We need three things, time, money, and help.

questions.explanation: Use a colon to introduce a list (three things: time, money, and help).

questions.question: Which is correct? (Two related sentences.)

options.a: It was late, we went home.
options.b: It was late; we went home.
options.c: It was late we went home.
options.d: It was late: we went home.

questions.explanation: Use a semicolon to join two closely related full sentences. A comma alone would be a run-on.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I think, that she is coming.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I think that she is coming.
I think that she's coming.
I think that she is coming
I think that she's coming
questions.explanation: No comma before 'that' in English in this pattern.

questions.question: Wrong: 'we went on monday.' Correct the sentence.
answers: We went on Monday.
We went on Monday
questions.explanation: Capitalise first word of sentence and the day of the week.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He and i left early.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He and I left early.
He and I left early
questions.explanation: The pronoun I is always written with a capital letter in English.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She speaks english and german.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She speaks English and German.
She speaks English and German
questions.explanation: Languages are capitalised in English.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I called but she was busy.' (Add a comma where needed.)
answers: I called, but she was busy.
I called, but she was busy
questions.explanation: Use a comma before 'but' when it joins two full sentences.

questions.question: Wrong: 'However we decided to stay.' Correct the sentence.
answers: However, we decided to stay.
However, we decided to stay
questions.explanation: Use a comma after introductory words like However.

questions.question: Wrong: 'In january we went to prague.' Correct the sentence.
answers: In January we went to Prague.
In January we went to Prague
questions.explanation: Capitalise months and place names (proper nouns).

questions.question: Wrong: 'The man, who stole the bag, was caught.' (The clause is essential – there were several men, and we mean the one who stole the bag.) Correct by removing unnecessary commas.
answers: The man who stole the bag was caught.
The man who stole the bag was caught
questions.explanation: No commas when the clause is defining (essential to identify which man).

questions.question: Wrong: 'after the meeting he went home.' Correct the sentence.
answers: After the meeting, he went home.
After the meeting, he went home
questions.explanation: Capitalise first word; comma after introductory phrase 'After the meeting'.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She said that, she would come.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She said that she would come.
She said that she would come
questions.explanation: No comma before or after 'that' in this type of sentence.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We need two things time and money.' Add the correct punctuation.
answers: We need two things: time and money.
We need two things: time and money
questions.explanation: Use a colon to introduce a list (two things: time and money).

questions.question: Wrong: 'The train was delayed we missed the meeting.' Join the two sentences with the correct punctuation.
answers: The train was delayed; we missed the meeting.
The train was delayed; we missed the meeting
questions.explanation: Use a semicolon between two closely related full sentences.

questions.question: Wrong: 'There was one problem the door was locked.' Add the correct punctuation.
answers: There was one problem: the door was locked.
There was one problem: the door was locked
questions.explanation: Use a colon to introduce an explanation (one problem: the door was locked).

questions.question: Which is correct? (List of places with commas inside.)

options.a: We visited Paris, France, Rome, Italy, and Berlin, Germany.
options.b: We visited Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Berlin, Germany.
options.c: We visited Paris, France: Rome, Italy: and Berlin, Germany.
options.d: We visited Paris; France, Rome; Italy, and Berlin; Germany.

questions.explanation: Use semicolons to separate list items when each item already contains a comma.
